bins.name: 20 MW threshold alignment
bins.desc: Fervo supports the 20 MW threshold as appropriately tailored and consistent with existing LGIP and LGIA frameworks, ensuring procedural consistency and predictability for large-load and hybrid customers.
quotes: a 20 MW threshold is reasonable, appropriately tailored to the types of high-impact projects this rulemaking is intended to address, and fully consistent with the structure of the existing pro forma LGIP and LGIA

The LGIP framework is the Commission's established mechanism for studying and processing generation interconnection requests that can materially affect transmission system reliability. Aligning the applicability threshold for large-load and hybrid interconnections with this framework can ensure procedural consistency, reduce administrative complexity for transmission providers, and create a predictable pathway for customers whose system impacts are similar to those of large generators.

bins.name: Commercial readiness requirements
bins.desc: Fervo supports readiness criteria that mirror generator standards, including site control and contracting milestones, to distinguish commercially viable projects and improve queue efficiency without imposing excessive financial barriers.
quotes: Fervo supports the Commission's focus on ensuring that large-load and hybrid interconnection customers demonstrate commercial readiness before entering and advancing through the interconnection queue. Fervo encourages the Commission to prioritize readiness criteria that mirror the maturity expectations already applied to large generator interconnection customers, including demonstrated site control or contracting milestones.

Applying a readiness framework to load and hybrid customers can help transmission providers distinguish commercially viable projects, improve queue efficiency, and reduce speculative requests without imposing unnecessary financial barriers.

bins.name: Proportionate system protection
bins.desc: Fervo supports tailored protection requirements that account for geothermal's stable operating profile and minimal system risk, rather than broadly prescriptive mandates that do not reflect geothermal's actual operational characteristics.
quotes: Fervo agrees that appropriate system-protection requirements may be necessary for hybrid facilities but emphasizes that such requirements should be proportionate to the actual operational characteristics and risks of the resource. Geothermal has stable and predictable operating profiles and does not exhibit fast-changing injection or withdrawal patterns that drive many protection concerns for other technologies.
protection systems for these hybrids can be tailored rather than broadly prescriptive, ensuring reliability without imposing unnecessary or overly burdensome technical mandates

Fervo encourages the Commission to ensure that any enforcement mechanisms or operational limits for unauthorized injections or withdrawals reflect the physical realities of hybrid geothermal operations and do not inadvertently constrain resources that already pose minimal system-protection risk.

bins.name: Expedited study pathway
bins.desc: Fervo supports expedited interconnection studies for hybrid facilities that offer operational flexibility (curtailability or dispatchability), as these characteristics reduce system impacts and accelerate deployment of clean firm resources.
quotes: Fervo supports the Commission's proposal to expedite interconnection studies for large loads and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable or dispatchable, as these operational flexibilities materially reduce system impacts and allow transmission providers to study such projects with greater confidence.

An expedited study process that recognizes the reliability value of curtailable or dispatchable hybrids would help accelerate deployment of clean firm resources while allowing transmission providers to prioritize projects that offer the greatest operational benefit.

bins.name: Hybrid framework and queue election
bins.desc: Fervo supports allowing existing generation projects to elect into the coordinated hybrid study framework without loss of queue priority, enabling early geothermal hybrid projects to benefit from new rules that reflect their reduced system impacts.
quotes: Fervo therefore recommends that generation projects already in the generation interconnection queue, or in late-stage development, be permitted to elect into the new coordinated hybrid study framework where doing so would better reflect these reduced system impacts.

Allowing transmission providers to apply the new modeling assumptions, hybrid treatment rules, and coordinated study processes to existing queue positions, without forcing restudy or loss of queue priority, would ensure that early geothermal hybrid projects can access the benefits of reforms intended for exactly this type of configuration.

bins.name: System impact-based cost allocation
bins.desc: Fervo supports cost-allocation rules that recognize when project design reduces system impact, rather than applying standardized costs that do not reflect actual system benefits or reflect net flows.
quotes: Fervo supports clear cost-allocation rules but emphasizes that any cost-allocation framework must recognize when a project's design meaningfully reduces its system impact.

Hybrid geothermal facilities that "bring their own power" can substantially decrease the need for upstream transmission reinforcements by co-locating firm, dispatchable generation with load, often avoiding or minimizing upgrades that a stand-alone load would otherwise require.

Fervo therefore encourages the Commission to ensure that cost responsibility is applied in a way that reflects actual system benefits and not over-assign costs to hybrids that reduce net flows or improve local reliability.

bins.name: Coordinated study framework
bins.desc: Fervo supports coordinated study processes and modeling frameworks that align large-load and hybrid interconnections with generator procedures, creating procedural consistency and enabling existing projects to retain queue benefits.
quotes: The LGIP framework is the Commission's established mechanism for studying and processing generation interconnection requests that can materially affect transmission system reliability. Aligning the applicability threshold for large-load and hybrid interconnections with this framework can ensure procedural consistency, reduce administrative complexity for transmission providers, and create a predictable pathway for customers whose system impacts are similar to those of large generators.

Fervo supports the Commission's focus on ensuring that large-load and hybrid interconnection customers demonstrate commercial readiness before entering and advancing through the interconnection queue. Fervo encourages the Commission to prioritize readiness criteria that mirror the maturity expectations already applied to large generator interconnection customers, including demonstrated site control or contracting milestones.

Applying a readiness framework to load and hybrid customers can help transmission providers distinguish commercially viable projects, improve queue efficiency, and reduce speculative requests without imposing unnecessary financial barriers.

Fervo therefore recommends that generation projects already in the generation interconnection queue, or in late-stage development, be permitted to elect into the new coordinated hybrid study framework where doing so would better reflect these reduced system impacts.

Allowing transmission providers to apply the new modeling assumptions, hybrid treatment rules, and coordinated study processes to existing queue positions, without forcing restudy or loss of queue priority, would ensure that early geothermal hybrid projects can access the benefits of reforms intended for exactly this type of configuration.

bins.name: Fair cost assignment for hybrids
bins.desc: Fervo supports cost allocation and crediting mechanisms that fairly reflect the transmission system benefits provided by hybrid geothermal facilities, avoiding cost over-assignment for projects that reduce net flows or improve local reliability.
quotes: Fervo supports clear cost-allocation rules but emphasizes that any cost-allocation framework must recognize when a project's design meaningfully reduces its system impact.

Fervo therefore encourages the Commission to ensure that cost responsibility is applied in a way that reflects actual system benefits and not over-assign costs to hybrids that reduce net flows or improve local reliability.
Where appropriate, crediting mechanisms should remain available to acknowledge these benefits and maintain fair treatment across different project types.

bins.name: Generation-load co-location benefits
bins.desc: Fervo emphasizes that co-locating firm, dispatchable generation with load substantially reduces transmission reinforcement needs and that transition rules and cost allocation should recognize these system benefits.
quotes: Hybrid geothermal facilities that "bring their own power" can substantially decrease the need for upstream transmission reinforcements by co-locating firm, dispatchable generation with load, often avoiding or minimizing upgrades that a stand-alone load would otherwise require.

Fervo supports the Commission's effort to establish a clear and orderly transition plan and encourages the Commission to design that transition to recognize the system benefits of projects that co-locate large loads with clean generation.

bins.name: Operational flexibility value
bins.desc: Fervo supports recognizing that curtailability and dispatchability materially reduce system impacts; geothermal-based hybrids are particularly well suited to provide predictable, operator-controlled responses that improve system reliability.
quotes: Fervo supports the Commission's proposal to expedite interconnection studies for large loads and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable or dispatchable, as these operational flexibilities materially reduce system impacts and allow transmission providers to study such projects with greater confidence.

Geothermal-based hybrid projects are particularly well suited for this approach because they can provide highly predictable, operator-controlled responses that improve system reliability during peak or contingency events.

An expedited study process that recognizes the reliability value of curtailable or dispatchable hybrids would help accelerate deployment of clean firm resources while allowing transmission providers to prioritize projects that offer the greatest operational benefit.

bins.name: Local system benefit recognition
bins.desc: Fervo supports cost responsibility frameworks that reflect actual local and systemic benefits from hybrids and allow existing projects to benefit from new rules without forced restudy when electing hybrid framework.
quotes: Fervo therefore encourages the Commission to ensure that cost responsibility is applied in a way that reflects actual system benefits and not over-assign costs to hybrids that reduce net flows or improve local reliability.

Allowing transmission providers to apply the new modeling assumptions, hybrid treatment rules, and coordinated study processes to existing queue positions, without forcing restudy or loss of queue priority, would ensure that early geothermal hybrid projects can access the benefits of reforms intended for exactly this type of configuration.

bins.name: Geothermal operating characteristics
bins.desc: Fervo describes geothermal as having stable, predictable operating profiles without fast-changing injection or withdrawal patterns, supporting proportionate rather than prescriptive protection and expedited study eligibility.
quotes: Fervo agrees that appropriate system-protection requirements may be necessary for hybrid facilities but emphasizes that such requirements should be proportionate to the actual operational characteristics and risks of the resource. Geothermal has stable and predictable operating profiles and does not exhibit fast-changing injection or withdrawal patterns that drive many protection concerns for other technologies.

Geothermal-based hybrid projects are particularly well suited for this approach because they can provide highly predictable, operator-controlled responses that improve system reliability during peak or contingency events.
